unique_issues.summary: 7-day no-follow-up label conflates true resolution with abandonment/churn, contaminating labels and evaluation.
Using the full article corpus to initialize preprocessing/retrieval artifacts leaks future information into BM25 and tokenizer/vocabulary statistics.
In-batch contrastive training biases learning toward popular articles because positives are frequency-skewed, harming rare-article performance.
Hyperparameter tuning on a single contiguous validation window risks temporal overfitting to that period.
Tickets with no clicked article are structurally unanswerable and make Hits@3 harder to interpret.
Transformer has more hyperparameters than BM25, so equal grid-search effort may still favor the transformer's validation overfitting risk.
Metadata features are handled asymmetrically, giving BM25 and the transformer different access to timestamps/category tags and potentially enabling shortcuts.
Future article content in the full 24-month corpus snapshot leaks into preprocessing artifacts and document encodings, causing temporal/preprocessing leakage.
Giving the transformer explicit timestamp metadata while BM25 cannot use comparable recency information creates an unfair structural advantage.
In-batch contrastive training without hard negatives may yield only coarse topic separation and creates a train/test difficulty mismatch relative to ranking against the full corpus.
Resolution labels based on absence of follow-up are noisy and category-dependent because different ticket categories resolve on different timescales.
The 7-day follow-up window is right-censored for tickets near the end of the test period, creating label noise and prevalence shift in the most recent test data.
The 500-ticket human annotation set is too small for reliable stratified estimates, and semantic relevance annotations do not match the primary resolution-enabling label construct.
BM25 cannot exploit structured metadata such as timestamps or category tags, while the transformer can, so the comparison conflates semantic understanding with metadata exploitation.
The resolution labels are derived from clicks on BM25-surfaced articles, so the positive label set is constrained by BM25's recall and may understate transformer gains.
Human raters judge semantic relevance rather than resolution likelihood, so human-model agreement cannot isolate whether transformer gains reflect true resolution improvement.
Using the full 24-month corpus snapshot to initialize preprocessing artifacts and retrieval statistics leaks future information into evaluation
The resolution label is confounded by agent expertise, routing, and documentation behavior rather than article quality alone
The 7-day no-follow-up heuristic is a survivorship/churn proxy that can correlate with customer engagement rather than semantic relevance
In-batch negatives in contrastive training are too easy or can suppress popular articles, creating a training artifact that may distort learning
The human annotation set is too small/underpowered for reliable validation of model lift or per-category analysis
The label construction mixes explicit link resolution with click-plus-no-follow-up, making the target reflect agent documentation practices unevenly across segments
Hits@3 as the primary metric is insensitive to ordering within the top 3, and McNemar's test on that binary metric cannot detect ranking-position degradation
The sentence-transformer dual encoder may not have adequate pretrained representations for support-ticket language, making the claimed semantic advantage unvalidated

Using a 7-day post-discharge utilization feature leaks information from inside the 30-day readmission label window, because it captures post-discharge events that are outcomes rather than true predictors.

The transformer is given raw 7-day utilization event streams while the baseline gets only an engineered summary, creating an unfair architecture comparison due to asymmetric access to the most predictive signal.
The hand-engineered sequence-feature baseline is underspecified, so a transformer win may reflect incomplete feature engineering rather than architectural superiority.
Treating AUPRC and calibration thresholds as separate pass/fail criteria without specifying how to handle mixed outcomes leaves the overall experimental verdict underspecified.

The transformer may have an advantage because it receives raw sequence features such as lab trajectories and medication sequences that were not extracted for the baseline, so the sensitivity analysis does not isolate architectural effects.
The 6-month test window may be too short to detect temporal degradation from calendar-time patterns learned during the 24-month training window, especially without month-by-month test reporting.
Stratifying by prior admission count may confound history length, label prevalence, and interpretability, making the proposed interpretation rule for the stratified analysis potentially backwards.

The transformer's 24-month lookback is truncated for early-window patients, creating non-stationary history availability that could let the model learn full-history-specific patterns and inflate test performance.
Early post-discharge 7-day utilization features may not be available at prediction time and leak post-discharge information tied to readmission risk.
The baseline lacks temporally ordered sequence inputs compared with the transformer, so performance differences may reflect feature richness rather than architectural sequence learning.
The stratified analysis for history-length confounding is logically inverted and does not support the stated interpretation about genuine sequence learning.
Forward-fill imputation within 48-hour windows may create documentation-density signals that the transformer can exploit more easily than the baseline.
Hyperparameter tuning on a near-future validation window may not transfer to a farther-future test window under temporal drift, and may affect the transformer differently than XGBoost.
The hand-engineered sequence feature sensitivity analysis lacks a pre-specified falsification threshold and cannot distinguish genuine sequence learning from spurious pattern learning.
The transformer sees raw post-discharge event sequences while the baseline sees only scalar summaries, creating an information asymmetry unrelated to sequence modeling ability.
A permuted-sequence ablation is needed to test whether transformer gains come from genuine sequence learning rather than feature engineering failure.
Early 7-day post-discharge utilization features may leak information available only after the prediction time and directly encode post-discharge processes tied to 30-day readmission.

The baseline sensitivity analysis is not comparable because the transformer has a much longer lookback/history window than the hand-engineered baseline, so performance gains may reflect more information rather than architecture.
The stratified confound analysis has incorrect directional logic; the proposed interpretation of which patient group should show larger gains is likely non-diagnostic or reversed.
AUPRC may be misleading if readmission prevalence shifts across the temporal train/test split, because comparisons are anchored to different baselines.
Hand-engineered baseline sequence features may be too sparse to establish the true non-transformer ceiling, so residual gains could still be recoverable by feature engineering.
AUPRC as the primary metric is less standard than AUROC for binary classification.
Pooling train/validation/test data to estimate preprocessing parameters (e.g., medians, clipping thresholds, categorical consolidation) leaks evaluation information into feature engineering.

Giving the text-augmented model full hyperparameter search while keeping the structured-only baseline fixed creates an unfair comparison where gains may come from optimization effort rather than text features.
The 72-hour admission label conflates same-encounter admissions with post-discharge return visits, mixing two different prediction tasks and distorting interpretation of text signal.
Seasonal stratification does not prevent within-period workflow or EHR documentation drift from shifting chief complaint behavior over time, leading to temporal distribution shift.
Feature importance inspection and arrival-mode stratification cannot reliably determine whether text embeddings encode independent symptoms versus clinician judgment about severity.
Pooled preprocessing statistics (medians, clipping thresholds, rare-category handling) are estimated using the full dataset including test data, causing leakage into feature engineering.
The structured baseline is not fairly tuned because it uses a fixed conservative hyperparameter configuration while the text-augmented model receives full hyperparameter search.
Stratified visit-level splitting lets repeat patients and future temporal patterns appear in both train and test, yielding an overly optimistic estimate for real deployment.
Claims that arrival-mode stratification, ablation, and feature importance can prove whether text encodes clinician judgment versus patient severity are not mechanistically sufficient.
Using tree-based feature importance under strong feature correlation is unreliable for attributing causal or mechanistic importance to text features.
AUROC is not the most relevant metric for an operational thresholded admission decision; performance at the target operating point matters more.
Classifying observation stays as negative outcomes may introduce label noise that could affect measured model performance.
The concern that text embeddings were generated on the full dataset before splitting is a leakage claim.
Stratifying by arrival mode may not adequately separate clinician judgment encoding from patient state encoding because confounders differ across strata.
The 72-hour admission label may incorporate post-triage information, so the apparent text gain could partly reflect information not available at true triage time.
Pooled preprocessing estimates computed on the full dataset leak held-out distribution information into feature engineering.
The text-augmented model receives hyperparameter tuning while the structured-only baseline is fixed or undertrained, making the comparison unfair.
Using the stratified-by-arrival-mode analysis to infer whether embeddings independently capture severity versus clinician judgment is not causally identifiable from that analysis.
The test set is used to choose between model configurations when validation and test results disagree, contaminating the held-out evaluation.
AUROC may not reflect clinically meaningful improvements at specific operational thresholds.
Multi-site documentation style differences may confound text signals and are not controlled by arrival-mode stratification.
A 72-hour admission label may misclassify some true positives, potentially in a way correlated with chief complaint text.
24-hour post-delivery support interactions and service-resolution signals are included as features for a 7-day escalation prediction, creating leakage because they are early markers of the outcome.
The baseline is fixed/untuned while the sequence model gets extensive hyperparameter search, making the comparison unfair because gains may reflect optimization budget rather than model structure.
ECE is used as the main selection metric in a very imbalanced setting, which can favor near-zero predictions and may select poorly performing models for the operational task.
The evaluation is restricted to customers with 6+ months of history, which narrows the population and limits generalizability to new customers.
Using a bidirectional LSTM in a real-time incremental prediction setting is non-deployable because it relies on future context.
24-hour post-delivery support/service-resolution features may leak outcome information because they are part of the escalation process window and can inflate performance.
Sequence model gets extensive hyperparameter tuning while the baseline uses a fixed configuration, making comparison unfair and attributing gains to tuning effort rather than architecture.
Using ECE as the primary metric emphasizes calibration rather than the operational goal of high-recall early escalation detection, so a well-calibrated model may still be ineffective.
Restricting evaluation to customers with at least 6 months of history excludes new customers and limits generalizability, likely inflating performance estimates.
The timestamp-shuffling ablation may preserve order and therefore tests timestamp dependence rather than true temporal ordering dependence.
Bidirectional LSTM may use future context from later sequence positions, which is not causally aligned with an early-triage deployment setting.
Bidirectional LSTM is mismatched to causal/autoregressive temporal prediction and does not cleanly isolate sequence-order effects.
Sequence model received extensive hyperparameter tuning while the baseline used a fixed configuration, confounding architecture with optimization effort.
Selecting the sequence model by minimizing ECE and then comparing models primarily on ECE introduces selection bias and overemphasizes calibration at low prevalence.
Different calendar start points and a 6+ month history filter make validation and test cohorts non-identical and may limit evaluation to established customers.
24-hour post-delivery support contact counts and service-resolution signals are leakage because they occur within the escalation process being predicted.
Model may be using event-ordering versus recency/time-gap magnitude signals; the randomization ablation cannot distinguish them.
Including 24-hour post-delivery support contact counts may dominate prediction and obscure whether temporal pattern learning is actually responsible.
Temporal split leaks future queries/content into training because splitting is stratified by calendar quarter with near-duplicate or interleaved queries, violating the temporal holdout structure.

Hard-negative mining may select genuinely relevant documents or overly BM25-favored documents as negatives, causing the model to learn divergence from BM25 or suppress relevant but lexically different documents.
Per-query AUROC is unstable or undefined when there are too few labeled documents per query, making the metric unreliable across queries.
Reusing the same clinician panel for validation and test annotation could introduce panel calibration drift that biases evaluation toward the selected model.
The transformer checkpoint may have inadequate coverage of portal-specific clinical terminology, causing out-of-vocabulary or representation failures on specialist queries.
In-batch negatives from temporally contiguous query logs may include false negatives because nearby queries can share clinical intent.
Query-level partitioning without deduplication allows repeated or near-identical queries to appear in both train and test, enabling memorization leakage.
In-batch negative training uses a batch-dependent negative distribution that may not match full-corpus inference, creating a train-test mismatch.
Hard-negative mining based on model or BM25 scores is circular and does not truly correct for position bias.
Document corpus drift over 24 months may disadvantage the neural encoder relative to BM25 on recently updated documents.
Random/non-temporal splitting permits future or near-duplicate query patterns and document-level click leakage into test data, overstating neural retrieval performance versus BM25.
Annotation-pool bias for AUROC: if evaluation pairs come only from BM25-exposed documents, the neural model is unfairly penalized for retrieving relevant documents outside the pool.
In-batch negatives create a train/test candidate-set mismatch because training uses batch-sized negatives while evaluation is over the full corpus.
Hard-negative mining may inject false negatives or suppress genuinely relevant documents when the initial encoder or click-log mining is unreliable in the clinical domain.
A small annotation budget (about 500 pairs) may yield unstable AUROC estimates, especially without multiple annotated documents per query.
The base transformer checkpoint may not adequately cover institutional clinical vocabulary, and this prerequisite is untested before tuning.
A small validation set may be insufficient to support a multi-dimensional hyperparameter sweep and a reliable single test-set claim.
Hard negatives mined from BM25 top results can make the neural model learn specifically to outrank BM25-promoted documents, creating a partially circular comparison.
Per-query AUROC on 500 pairs and subgroup analyses across 12 strata are underpowered, with no power calculations or confidence intervals.
Query-level AUROC is the wrong metric for clinical retrieval because it does not measure top-k ranking quality, especially the first 10 results physicians inspect.
Random query-level splitting can place semantically near-duplicate queries in both train and test, inflating encoder performance through leakage-like memorization.
Hard-negative mining from clicked/non-clicked or BM25-surfaced documents can encode position bias and teach the model to suppress documents that may actually be relevant.
Only 500 annotated pairs spread across many queries likely yields too few judgments per query, making per-query AUROC estimates noisy and the macro-averaged result underpowered.
Checkpoint choice between generic and clinically adapted BERT is an uncontrolled confounder that could explain gains independently of click-log fine-tuning.
AUROC is misaligned with the retrieval objective because it measures overall discrimination rather than top-k ranking quality in the first few results physicians actually inspect.

Evaluation pairs drawn from interaction logs may cover only documents BM25 already surfaced, so AUROC measures re-ranking rather than full-corpus retrieval and misses gains on lexically distant relevant documents.
Stratified random splitting by calendar quarter can leak future document text and behavior into training, violating temporal ordering and inflating future performance estimates.
Evaluation restricted to BM25-surfaced documents may hide the neural model's advantage on semantically similar but lexically distant documents.
Graph/sender-reputation features are computed on the full corpus before splitting, leaking information from test data into training and test features.
The joint model gets a full hyperparameter search while the text-only baseline uses a fixed configuration, confounding architecture gains with optimization effort.
The held-out test set is used for checkpoint selection or tie-breaking, compromising its unbiased role.
The message-level stratified split across the 18-month corpus causes temporal leakage / future-data contamination.
Conservative labeling excludes stealthy or low-confirmation attacks from the positive class, biasing evaluation against cases where graph features help most.
Fixed vocabulary/tokenization from the full corpus before splitting is harmless leakage, but the 5,000-token cutoff creates an unacknowledged OOV ceiling on novel phishing vocabulary.
ROC-AUC is an inappropriate primary metric for severely imbalanced phishing detection because it can obscure operational false-positive costs.
Stratified message-level splitting leaks campaign/future sender behavior across splits, inflating generalization estimates.
Sender reputation / graph statistics are computed over the full corpus before splitting, leaking future/test information into graph features.
The text-only baseline is under-tuned relative to the joint model, so the comparison confounds architecture with optimization effort.
Using the held-out test partition to break checkpoint ties or choose the most stable checkpoint invalidates the test set as an unbiased holdout.
ROC-AUC is the wrong primary metric for a severely imbalanced phishing task with low-FPR operational constraints.
Keyword/vocabulary refinement may have used corpus text that included test messages, leaking information into text features.
Conservative labeling leaves unlabeled positives in the negative class, which may distort evaluation, especially when combined with graph features.
Stratified message-level splitting leaks campaign/sender/time-correlated information across train and test, inflating apparent generalization for a temporally ordered phishing task.
Graph features such as sender reputation and first-time-sender indicators are computed using the full corpus before splitting, leaking test-period information into training features.
The baseline is given a fixed hyperparameter configuration while the joint model gets hyperparameter search, so the comparison confounds architecture with optimization effort.
Conservative labeling leaves an unquantified set of unlabeled positives in the negative class, biasing training and evaluation.
The held-out test set is used to choose a checkpoint or tie-break among checkpoints, causing test-set peeking and optimistic bias.
Conservative labeling systematically under-labels novel-campaign positives, biasing subgroup analysis against new-sender cases.
ROC-AUC is an inappropriate primary metric for a severely imbalanced phishing-detection setting with low-false-positive operational goals.
Training treats unlabeled alerts as negatives, which can corrupt the negative class with latent positives and penalize useful signal.
The 72-hour confirmation window creates boundary truncation near split edges, under-labeling true incidents around month cutoffs.
Cluster-level deduplication in evaluation but not training creates a mismatch that may let the ranker exploit cluster members while MAP@20 counts them as one finding.
Frozen DistilBERT embeddings on templated SIEM text are an unvalidated prerequisite; if they add no signal beyond rule ID and severity, the text advantage is unsubstantiated.
Cluster-adjusted MAP@20 is misaligned with the individual-alert pairwise training objective, so the model may not optimize the primary metric.
The primary use of MAP@20 is misaligned with the operational goal of strict top-k precision for small analyst triage capacity.
72-hour confirmation window creates boundary ambiguity and potential split-related label leakage/bias around train/validation/test boundaries.
Cluster-level MAP adjustment is underspecified and depends on an unvalidated clustering component, so ranking performance is confounded with clustering quality and may vary by convention.
Frozen DistilBERT on templated enterprise alert text may yield near-constant or non-informative embeddings, making the text channel ineffective for testing the value of alert text.
Treating unlabeled alerts as negatives can inject capacity/queue-position bias because analyst non-review is not equivalent to true negative status.
The experiment conflates the contribution of text signals with the contribution of the ranking formulation because it lacks a 2x2 ablation separating these factors.
Target encoding may leak a stale incident-rate prior and mislead the model under distribution shift for categorical rule families.
Training labels are capacity-biased by the 72-hour confirmation process, so the model may learn existing triage priority rather than true incident relevance.
The pairwise training objective is misaligned with cluster-adjusted MAP@20 evaluation, so optimizing loss may not improve the reported metric.
Assumed stationarity over the 18-month data window may be violated by changes in SIEM rules, analyst workflow, or confirmation thresholds.
Selection bias / non-random missing labels from analyst triage and queue prioritization
DistilBERT embeddings on SIEM/security alert text may be uninformative or untested for this domain, making the text-signal hypothesis unassessed
Cluster-level MAP@20 adjustment is underspecified / potentially inconsistent across multi-day campaigns or daily vs global cluster definitions
Secondary analysis confounds model capacity with methodology by comparing different model classes instead of holding classifier architecture constant
Cluster-level deduplication or adjustment applied only at evaluation, not training, creates train/eval objective mismatch
72-hour confirmation window interacts with split boundaries, causing asymmetric label truncation near dataset edges
Daily-batch pairwise training ignores zero-incident days, biasing evaluation/training toward high-prevalence days
Three-month test window may be too short to reflect seasonal or campaign-specific variance
Primary use of MAP@20 is misaligned with the operational objective of strict top-k triage precision at k=5-10
24-hour escalation/ticket linkage label measures analyst workflow and prioritization rather than verified intrusion ground truth, creating proxy-label mismatch and survivorship bias
Ticket-history features may be correlated with the label or with active investigator workflow, so gains may come from identifying already-watched entities rather than intrinsic alert risk
Hyperparameter tuning on a 3-month validation window may overfit short-term alert distributions and not transfer to the longer test period under drift
Static graph/entity-store features may leak future relationships into earlier alerts if the store is not built with a strict rolling window
nDCG@10 alone may miss catastrophic failures where a true alert is ranked below the top 10, so Recall@10 should also be reported
Frozen generic SBERT embeddings may be weak on SIEM alert text, making the text-only baseline too weak a comparator

Label construction uses analyst escalation or incident-ticket linkage within 24 hours, so the target reflects workflow response rather than confirmed intrusion truth; this makes evaluation measure analyst prioritization instead of true incident relevance.

Ticket-history features leak label information / create correlation with the positive class because prior incident-ticket activity on an entity is itself part of the same escalation process used to define labels, leading to memorization of entity risk or incident membership rather than alert-level discrimination.
The static entity store may have been populated retroactively or built from the full time range, causing future information to leak into graph features despite a stated lookback window.
Using nDCG@10 for hyperparameter selection may be unstable in a sparse-label setting, especially if query construction is unspecified and there are very few relevant items per query.

The static entity store / graph topology may be built once from the full 24-month period rather than point-in-time per alert, which would leak future infrastructure relationships into training features.
nDCG@10 can misrepresent operational ranking quality when alert volume varies across shifts, because it measures different fractions of the queue under different daily loads.
The 24-hour escalation window may create time-of-day label bias if analyst shift timing affects escalation probability, which features correlated with time-of-day could exploit.

Ticket-history features may leak label information by encoding analyst escalation or prior incident-ticket linkage, inflating apparent performance on entities with existing positive-label history and hurting generalization to new campaigns.
Dropping alerts with no disposition within 30 days biases the dataset toward a higher positive rate and away from operationally relevant low-priority/late-disposition cases.
The entity graph may suffer from temporal leakage if graph relationships are not time-indexed per alert and include future information.
The ablation design is insufficient to isolate the independent contribution of entity-graph features versus ticket-history features because it lacks the full comparison matrix.
Reporting a single aggregate nDCG@10 over a long non-stationary test window can conflate ranking quality with temporal drift.
The label definition uses analyst escalation or incident ticket linkage within 24 hours, which evaluates analyst workflow/proxy outcomes rather than verified true intrusions.
Hyperparameter selection on a 3-month validation window may not transfer to a 6-month test horizon under distribution shift.
nDCG@10 query construction is underspecified because varying queue depths across shifts can change what 'top-10 per shift' means operationally.
Full-corpus preprocessing (event vocabulary and quantile bins) leaks future/test distribution into training representations, biasing evaluation on unseen attacks.
Claiming strict chronological ordering while also stratifying within splits by attack type is internally inconsistent and may require temporal leakage or campaign mixing.
The gradient-boosted baseline is under-optimized relative to the transformer, so the comparison may confound model choice with tuning effort.
ROC-AUC is a poor primary metric for severely imbalanced intrusion detection with operational precision constraints and may misrepresent useful performance.
Using the held-out test set to break ties in model selection compromises test independence and biases reported performance.
Treating unlabeled or uninvestigated events inconsistently in training/evaluation can bias the model toward investigated incidents and inflate reported precision/recall.
Full-corpus preprocessing (vocabulary/quantile bins) leaks validation/test information into training and can inflate out-of-distribution performance.
The baseline is intentionally under-tuned / fixed while the transformer receives hyperparameter search, making the comparison unfair.
Chronological splitting combined with attack-type stratification may require reordering or recycling examples across temporal boundaries, risking temporal leakage.
ROC-AUC is an inappropriate primary metric under severe class imbalance and operational false-alert constraints; PR-style metrics would be more relevant.
Using the test set to break ties in model selection compromises test independence and biases performance estimates upward.
Full-corpus preprocessing statistics like vocabulary and quantile bins are computed using test-period data, leaking future distribution information into feature construction.
The baseline is fixed/under-tuned while the transformer receives hyperparameter search, making the comparison unfair.
Attack-type stratification after a chronological split may reintroduce temporal leakage or undermine the integrity of the time-based test split.
Using the held-out test set to break model-selection ties compromises the independence of the test set.
Selecting models primarily by ROC-AUC is problematic under severe class imbalance and false-alert constraints because it may not reflect precision or operational utility.
The novel-vs-familiar attack sensitivity analysis may confound campaign novelty with feature-distribution shift.
Using 72-hour no-escalation as a negative label bakes analyst omissions and workload-driven censoring into ground truth.
Query grouping is underspecified (per shift vs per host), which can create degenerate NDCG computation and weak training signals.
The 60-second pre-alert telemetry window assumes relevant evidence is time-local to the alert, which may miss slow-burn activity.
BM25 is not the operational incumbent baseline; it may be a strawman compared with vendor severity or rule-based priority.
Excluding ambiguous-disposition alerts creates a cleaner-than-production evaluation set and may inflate precision.
Host risk score and open-incident count may leak future information if computed from retrospectively confirmed tickets rather than point-in-time logs.
Temporal cross-validation may leak future information unless folds are strictly forward-chained.
The model may gain lift mainly from host-risk history features, but no host-risk-only baseline is included to isolate that contribution.
BM25 is operationally underspecified because no query string is defined, and vendor-assigned priority is the true incumbent baseline.
The validation gap in temporal CV is much smaller than the deployment gap, so hyperparameters may be tuned for the wrong shift regime.
Query-group definition is underspecified, making NDCG@10 ambiguous and potentially invalid if grouping is not applied consistently across models and baselines.
The labeling protocol excludes long-dwell or unreviewed alerts, creating label noise/survivorship bias and misaligning evaluation with the operational goal.
Host risk score / historical incident count feature may leak future information or encode analyst decisions, causing temporal leakage or memorization of repeat offenders.
BM25 is an inadequate or non-comparable baseline because it is not a proper ranking model for the task and/or omits key feature modalities; a stronger same-input baseline is needed.
Temporal cross-validation may assume stationarity and hyperparameter selection stability that is not demonstrated.
The evaluation suffers from survivorship bias because positive labels depend on analyst discovery, so the model may learn analyst behavior rather than true incidents.
The 7-day open incident count feature may be computed using retrospective full-dataset information rather than alert-time information, causing temporal leakage.
NDCG@10 is misaligned with the stated objective of minimizing time-to-triage by surfacing the first urgent incident.
Novel-rule test subset may be too small to support powered conclusions about generalization to unseen rules.

NDCG@10 is misaligned with the operational goal of minimizing time-to-triage / surfacing the first urgent incident, since it rewards overall ranking quality rather than prioritizing the first critical alert.
The label 'never escalated within 72 hours' conflates true negatives with analyst capacity constraints and missed incidents, making labels reflect historical triage behavior rather than ground truth.
The query grouping for LambdaMART is underspecified (per shift vs. per host), which makes the ranking setup and NDCG aggregation ambiguous and potentially unstable for small groups.
The BM25 baseline is not operationally comparable because the query construction / formulation is unspecified, so it may not represent a meaningful current-practice baseline.
The 30-day host risk score and similar host-history features may leak label information or enable host memorization because they are structurally correlated with prior confirmed incidents.
Ambiguous-disposition alerts are excluded from training/evaluation, which may make the dataset unrepresentative of deployment conditions and inflate reported metrics.
Hyperparameters selected via short-window temporal cross-validation may not generalize to the much longer held-out evaluation windows, creating a selection/evaluation mismatch.
Fold-level performance variability within temporal cross-validation is not reported, so the reliability of hyperparameter selection cannot be assessed.
Vendor-assigned severity scores, which reflect current operational practice, are missing as a baseline.
Using non-clicked impressions as non-relevant labels can introduce exposure/position bias and circular evaluation when negatives are drawn from a baseline candidate set.
Primary use of impression-level pooled AUROC can overweight head queries and may not reflect per-query ranking quality.
Claiming a 4-month test window represents a full seasonal cycle is incorrect and may limit generalization beyond that window.
DistilBERT's pre-training/domain coverage was unverified, so results could depend on whether the pre-trained model already matches marketplace vocabulary.
Early stopping or tuning budgets may be asymmetric across models and could stop the transformer before convergence.
A 30-second dwell-time threshold for composite relevance labels is arbitrary, and traffic-mix drift between train/test can shift label distributions.
Using AUROC as the evaluation metric may favor the transformer because it matches binary cross-entropy training better than LambdaMART's ranking objective.
The 4-month test slice may overlap with similar seasonal patterns in training, inflating apparent generalization.
Comparing pre-trained DistilBERT fine-tuning against a non-randomized baseline does not separate pre-training benefits from fine-tuning methodology.
Debiased evaluation without debiased training leaves MNAR position bias in the labels/modeling pipeline and may explain transformer gains.
Pooled AUROC can hide poor tail-query behavior; macro-averaged query-level AUROC is missing.
Calling the 4-month window a full seasonal cycle is false because it covers at most two seasons.
Model selection is asymmetric because DistilBERT is early-stopped on validation loss while LambdaMART is tuned on validation AUROC.
Using impression-level AUROC as the main evaluation metric mismatches the ranking task and can reward global discrimination without improving within-query top-k ranking quality.
Treating all non-clicked impressions as non-relevant creates position-bias-induced label noise and false negatives correlated with rank.
The transformer's binary cross-entropy training objective is not directly comparable to the baseline's ranking objective, creating an asymmetric comparison.
DistilBERT's external pretraining data is an uncontrolled confound that may explain performance differences independently of the model architecture or fine-tuning.
Claiming that a 4-month test window captures a full seasonal cycle is factually incorrect.
Weak-supervision labels inherited from the production ranker's presentation bias may cause the transformer to reproduce logging artifacts rather than learn better semantics.
Early stopping with patience=3 may stop transformer training too early and bias the comparison against the transformer.
Head queries may dominate impression-level AUROC, so using it for model selection could hurt tail-query performance.
Using AUROC as the main evaluation metric for a ranking task instead of a ranking metric like NDCG@5 can misalign model selection with top-k search quality.
Labeling all non-clicked impressions as non-relevant introduces position/popularity bias from the logging ranker into both training and evaluation labels.
The claim that the 4-month test window spans a full seasonal cycle is internally inconsistent and does not actually cover a full year of seasonality.
No pre-specified rule determines whether AUROC or NDCG@5 decides which model 'wins,' creating post-hoc metric selection ambiguity.
Early stopping with patience=3 may be calibrated differently across models and could stop the transformer before convergence.
Lack of a domain-adapted encoder baseline prevents separating architecture effects from pretraining-domain mismatch.
Impression-level AUROC can be dominated by head queries with many impressions, obscuring per-query ranking quality.
The transformer and LambdaMART are compared on asymmetric feature sets, with LambdaMART receiving categorical attributes that the transformer does not see.
Early stopping with patience=3 on a large dataset may be misconfigured and risk under-training or epoch-length ambiguity, especially hurting tail-query generalization.
Random session-level splitting leaks future sessions into training/testing, causing temporal leakage and inflated performance estimates under deployment drift.
The proposed model changes both algorithm class and feature representation relative to the baseline, so the gain cannot be attributed specifically to sequence features.
Flattened sequence features for gradient-boosted trees may fail to capture event order and may be sensitive to padding/session-length artifacts; order sensitivity is not directly validated.
The 7-day label window may interact with promotional timing in a way that is not fully controlled by marginal promotion stratification.

High-cardinality product-ID features in gradient-boosted trees may encourage memorization of frequent SKUs or be ignored for sparse SKUs, so the model may reflect popularity bias rather than sequential intent.
Sequence truncation at 50 events may interact nonlinearly with session length, undermining confound control in long sessions.
Using the validation set to break ties between candidate configurations constitutes test-set reuse and biases the reported estimate upward.
Stratified random session-level split across time mixes future sessions into training/evaluation, causing temporal leakage and optimistic estimates under seasonality/concept drift.

The comparison confounds model class with feature representation because the sequence model and baseline differ simultaneously; a GBDT-on-baseline-features control is needed to isolate the effect of sequence features.
Gradient-boosted trees are order-agnostic, so without a specified sequence encoding the model may not actually use event order and may only exploit aggregate distributional statistics.
Using the test set as a tie-breaker for configuration selection makes test outcomes part of model selection and biases the final reported performance.
Raw product IDs are high-cardinality and non-stationary, so unseen products in test may be treated as out-of-vocabulary and degrade performance on new-product sessions.
Gradient-boosted trees on flat vectors require an unspecified truncation/padding scheme, and this representation choice is not ablated.
Fixed-length truncation at 50 events may discard important signal from long, high-intent sessions, especially where sequential patterns matter most.
Random stratified split over 18 months causes temporal leakage and over-optimistic evaluation due to mixing future and past sessions.
The comparison confounds model class and feature set because there is no GBM baseline trained on aggregate features only.
Product ID encoding for GBM is underspecified and could leak target statistics if computed using information beyond the training fold.
Truncating sequences to 50 events is underspecified (head vs. tail) and may discard informative late-session events.
Raw product ID features may perform well under random splits but degrade under temporal deployment because of catalog churn and seasonal effects.
The model comparison is confounded because the proposed approach uses both a more expressive model class and richer features than the baseline.
Using the test set as a tiebreaker for model selection biases the reported test metric upward.
Asymmetric propensity weighting is not a proper inverse-propensity correction and may bias learning differently across models, especially the higher-capacity transformer.
Restricting training and evaluation to items shown by the prior ranker creates candidate-set selection / truncation bias and measures reranking only within a closed world.
Training objective and evaluation metric are misaligned: listwise loss with in-batch negatives does not directly optimize per-impression AUROC on the displayed candidate pool.
Frozen popularity decile features may hurt the baseline under distribution shift while leaving the transformer unaffected, confounding representation quality with feature staleness.
Temporal retraining on months 1–15 after validation on months 13–15 creates a proximity advantage to the test set and may inflate test estimates.
Macro-aggregated per-impression AUROC overweights tail queries with small candidate sets and high variance, potentially obscuring where gains occur.
The manual relevance sample may lack a specified stratified sampling design, limiting its ability to detect position-bias confounds in tail queries.
The transformer gets a richer session representation from full prior-click product IDs than the baseline, so the comparison does not isolate text encoding alone.
Displayed-item training/annotation candidate pools may be biased by prior ranking, making evaluation dependent on non-independent shown items.
Macro-averaged AUROC over queries with tiny candidate sets can be high-variance and overly influenced by tail queries.
Hard negative mining / in-batch negatives are not symmetric across models, so the transformer and GBDT are trained on different negative distributions.
Retraining on combined train+validation data introduces an unresolved evaluation regime issue because the final model is not validated under the actual training setup.
Propensity weighting is applied asymmetrically, correcting only some labels/positions and leaving residual position bias.
Manual relevance validation sampled 5% of queries without stratifying by model disagreement, reducing power to detect disagreement-specific confounds.
Macro AUROC weights tail and head queries equally, so gains concentrated in low-volume tail queries may be operationally unimportant.
The experiment lacks a baseline using frozen pretrained BERT embeddings with GBDT, so it cannot isolate architecture improvements from text representation improvements.
Propensity weighting is applied asymmetrically or in a way that may distort labels and leave residual position/popularity bias in click data.
AUROC is computed on the prior-ranker-filtered candidate pool, which may induce survivorship/pre-filtering bias and compress discriminative range.
The comparison is confounded because the proposed model differs from the baseline in multiple ways at once, so gains cannot be attributed specifically to the transformer/joint encoder.
A stronger intermediate baseline is missing to isolate representation quality from architecture/capacity differences, such as a frozen-BERT plus GBDT or other neural-feature baseline.
The evaluation uses AUROC as the primary metric, which is misaligned with top-k search relevance and early-position business goals.
The test window may contain concept drift or temporal heterogeneity, so gains could be concentrated in sub-periods rather than stable over time.
Training negatives may be drawn from a pool containing post-training catalog entries, creating a possible temporal leakage path.
Late-period searches have truncated purchase-label windows, producing heterogeneous label quality across the test period.
Manual relevance annotation covers only a small, likely unstratified sample and may be underpowered for tail-query or position-bias-sensitive strata.
New-product distribution shift may make the transformer embeddings stale for items introduced after training.
In-batch negative mining may inject co-occurrence structure into the learned representations in a way the baseline and ablations do not control for.
AUROC on a pre-filtered candidate set measures discrimination within the displayed pool rather than true full-space ranking quality.
Preprocessing was fit on the full 18-month corpus before splitting, leaking future/test-period distributional information into training features.

The 24-hour labeling window/backward label linkage can assign positive labels to earlier sessions based on later analyst confirmations, creating label leakage or label-semantic mismatch around split boundaries and early-stage sessions.
The comparison does not isolate transformer architecture from access to sequential packet data, because no sequence-model baseline or equivalent ablation is included.
Five-fold temporal cross-validation over the 3-month validation window likely yields too few positive incidents per fold, making pAUC-based hyperparameter selection unstable and noisy.
Index-based positional encodings may be misaligned with variable-rate packet sequences, potentially harming slow-and-low beaconing patterns.
Removing host identifiers does not cleanly isolate sequence modeling if the ablation is only inference-time masking rather than retraining from scratch.
The primary metric pAUC-ROC is misaligned with the operational requirement to control false positives and alert fatigue, as it can reward higher recall at the expense of precision.
Preprocessing transformers were fit on the full 18-month corpus before temporal splitting, leaking future distributional information into training/validation.

The identity-ablation transformer is not a clean test of sequence modeling because hyperparameters were tuned on the identifier-inclusive model / the ablation does not isolate architecture effects from access to host identity or related host-history proxies.
Including host identifiers in the transformer lets it exploit host reputation/identity shortcuts, and no analysis was done on hosts unseen during training.
Temporal cross-validation folds are short and potentially misaligned with campaign timescales, making pAUC-based hyperparameter selection noisy and unstable.
Stratified i.i.d. bootstrap on temporally clustered intrusion data yields anticonservative confidence intervals because it ignores autocorrelation.
The 24-hour labeling/confirmation window induces asymmetric directional label noise, with some not-yet-confirmed attack sessions mislabeled as benign.
The primary comparison is ambiguous because the transformer gets host identifiers while the baseline does not.
Historical alert count on the source host is a proxy for host identity that remains even after removing explicit identifiers, so the ablation does not fully eliminate identity information.
Analyst-confirmed labels may introduce survivorship bias toward already-detectable attacks, excluding evasive APT behaviors.
Hyperparameters tuned on 72-day cross-validation folds may not transfer well to the 10-month training run because of train-size mismatch.
The metric pAUC-ROC is misaligned with the operational goal of controlling alert fatigue, since it can reward false-positive inflation rather than high precision with acceptable recall.
Preprocessing normalization/imputation/statistics were fit on the full 18-month corpus before splitting, leaking future test-period information into training/validation.

The transformer ablation is not a clean test of sequence modeling because the compared setups differ in feature granularity and/or model class, so any gain may reflect extra information or stronger capacity rather than sequence effects alone.
The temporal cross-validation over months 11–13 is ambiguous/internally inconsistent, and the small per-fold sample sizes may make pAUC-based hyperparameter selection noisy.
Session-level FPR / pAUC-ROC does not reflect analyst workload because false positives may cluster and require deduplication or host-level aggregation to map to investigation volume.
The 24-hour labeling window may mislabel slow-developing APT sessions as benign during training, creating systematic false negatives for the targeted threat class.
Class imbalance weighting is specified for the transformer but not explicitly for the GBM baseline, creating an asymmetric comparison that may disadvantage the baseline.
IPW propensity scores estimated from the baseline ranker/CTR policy may misweight examples the transformer would rank differently, biasing training away from queries where session context helps most.

The ablation does not isolate the value of session context because it confounds transformer architecture/text-representation gains with session-feature gains; a stronger control such as a GBDT with session features or a full three-way comparison is missing.

The 5,000 human-judged evaluation sample may underrepresent tail queries and has unclear per-query coverage, making subgroup conclusions and nDCG estimates vulnerable to sampling or unjudged-item bias.
Using a bidirectional session encoder trained on complete sessions could leak future clicks into earlier-query rankings, causing offline metric inflation that would not be available at serving time.
Human relevance labels are assigned without session context, so they cannot directly validate the claimed mechanism that session context improves relevance.
Session-initial queries receive empty session-context input, and the design does not check whether gains are concentrated only in mid-session queries with prior clicks.
Model selection on validation MRR using IPW-corrected click labels may be misaligned with final evaluation on human-judged MRR because the label distributions differ.
The 5,000 human-judged query-result pairs may not come from the same test-split period as the operational regime, creating a coverage mismatch.
Using propensity scores derived from the baseline ranker's exposure/click policy can create circular, policy-specific label bias or entanglement in transformer training.
The ablation design cannot isolate the effect of session context from differences in model capacity or end-to-end BERT fine-tuning versus the GBDT baseline.
The human-labeled 5,000-pair evaluation set may be underpowered for tail-query or mid/late-session subgroup analysis, so subgroup effects may be undetectable.
Using MRR as the primary metric/model-selection criterion is misaligned with the stated goal of improving top-10 ranking quality and discards graded relevance information.
Sessions with zero prior clicks feed all-padding input to the session encoder, so a masking bug could silently break performance on those sessions.
The claim that session-context gains can be established from the transformer-vs.-no-session ablation lacks a pre-registered threshold separating architecture effects from session-context effects.
IPW propensities estimated from a session-unaware baseline may fail to remove bias for the session-context signal and can leak baseline relevance into training labels
Human judgment without session context cannot assess session-dependent relevance for ambiguous queries, making the test set insensitive to the claimed mechanism
The transformer-vs-GBDT/baseline comparison cannot isolate session context because architectural/pretraining/text-representation advantages may explain the gap
The session encoder may be acting as a popularity or personalization signal rather than an intent-disambiguation mechanism
The labeled evaluation set is not stratified by session complexity or reformulation rate, potentially underrepresenting the cases where session context should help
A paired t-test on bounded reciprocal-rank scores may be unreliable without a power analysis
Residual position bias remains because aggregate position-level CTR does not fully deconfound exposure from relevance sorting
Session click sequences may encode user identity or personalization rather than query intent, so gains may not reflect context-based disambiguation
MRR is the primary metric even though the hypothesis targets nDCG@10 and graded top-10 relevance, so the evaluation metric is misaligned with the stated goal
MRR discards graded relevance judgments and may miss partial improvements across the top-10 list

Baseline uses aggregated summaries while transformer uses finer-grained alert tokens, so the comparison confounds representation granularity/aggregation choice with architecture; a middle baseline on transformer embeddings is needed.

The 30-day disposition or incident-label filter creates survivorship/survival bias by retaining only easy-to-adjudicate or analyst-notable positives, skewing both training and evaluation toward high-signal cases.

Host ID embeddings or memorized host/subnet history can leak entity identity across chronological splits, inflating within-organization test performance without proving transfer to unseen infrastructure.
MAP@10 averaged over 24-hour buckets is dominated by days with many incidents and may not reflect the sparse, operationally common days that drive analyst workload.

The host state summary may include containment/quarantine/isolation actions whose update timing is unclear, creating potential future-information leakage or soft lookahead from response-state features.
Retrospective ticket-ID assignment for incident-aware splitting may remove late-window positives from training and distort the apparent positive rate near the train/validation boundary.
Host-state summary may leak downstream analyst containment actions such as quarantine/isolation flags, creating temporal label leakage
MAP@10 over 24-hour windows may be misleading when zero-positive windows are included or excluded ambiguously, and is sensitive to window composition and alert volume
Transformer's sequence-modeling advantage may not materialize if the relevant multi-step kill-chain patterns are not recoverable within the 512-alert, 24-hour context window
Baseline and transformer may not receive equivalent information, so the comparison cannot isolate sequence modeling from richer input representations
The 30-day disposition filter may exclude complex multi-stage incidents and create survivorship bias, making the evaluated population easier than production
Host IDs unseen during training are mapped to OOV embeddings, which may hurt performance on new hosts
MAP@10 by 24-hour buckets is sensitive to the chosen granularity and varying alert volumes, making the aggregate metric hard to interpret
MAP@10 per 24-hour window may be dominated by atypical days with multiple incidents and may not align with analyst-effort or severity-weighted objectives
Sequence truncation at 512 alerts per host may silently degrade performance on high-volume hosts by dropping later diagnostic alerts
Rolling host-state summary includes containment/response flags like quarantine or isolation that are set after incident confirmation, leaking downstream incident-response information into features.
Baseline features cannot represent alert sequence ordering or co-occurrence structure, so the comparison may conflate model representational power with the value of learned sequences.
MAP@10 averaged over 24-hour windows may be dominated by high-incident days and mix early-detection performance with ongoing-incident ranking, inflating results via autocorrelation.
The 30-day disposition filter excludes hard-to-classify alerts and biases the evaluation set toward easier cases where handcrafted features already perform well.
MAP@10 may effectively behave like a hit-rate metric under the alert volume and positive rate, making it insensitive to ranking-quality differences between models.
The transformer may memorize organization-specific entity patterns such as host IDs, subnets, or rule sequences, making internal validity of the claimed sequence-learning advantage uncertain.
Per-day MAP@10 averaging is unstable under low and variable incident density and may be dominated by a few high-incident days or one incident cluster.
Checkpoint selection uses weak click-based labels while final evaluation uses strong explicit labels, so the chosen checkpoint may not be optimal for the test metric.
Hard negatives mined from BM25 train the transformer specifically against BM25's failure modes, making the BM25-vs-transformer comparison asymmetric.
The annotated evaluation queries are drawn from historical production logs / prior-system-conditioned sessions, creating selection bias and possible leakage from the prior system.
The lab context feature provides session-level history unavailable to BM25, confounding model architecture with extra feature access.
The 6-month lookback may include multiple clinical sessions and potentially leak training-set queries into test-time context.
It is ambiguous whether the 2,000 annotated queries were restricted to test-split sessions, so session-level partitioning of the gold evaluation set may not have been respected.
Using MRR@10 as the primary metric discards lower-ranked relevant documents and collapses graded relevance into a binary notion, potentially missing gains on somewhat relevant results.
Checkpoint selection is done with weak implicit/click labels while final evaluation uses strong expert labels, creating a misaligned validation target and potential model selection bias.
Hard negatives are mined from BM25, so the transformer is trained to correct BM25's specific errors, which can inflate the apparent transformer-vs-BM25 performance gap.
Using a 6-month prior-query lab context could leak information across temporal splits if sessions are not chronologically ordered, causing future interactions to influence training and evaluation.
If the explicit annotation pool is drawn from documents surfaced by the incumbent system, novel transformer results may be unjudged and thus unfairly penalized.

The transformer uses richer input features such as specialty tokens, MeSH terms, and lab context that BM25 does not see, so the comparison conflates feature advantages with semantic retrieval capability.
Clinician relevance judgments may be anchored to the prior system's retrieval distribution, biasing the gold standard toward the incumbent system's preferences.
Training on BM25-mined hard negatives makes the model a corrective residual over BM25 and structurally inflates the comparison against BM25.
Static corpus shared across temporal splits may let later-indexed documents be encoded during training even when they only appear in test queries, though this is likely a weak concern.
Checkpoint selection is optimized on weak implicit click labels while evaluation uses strong explicit relevance labels, so the selected model may be misaligned with the test metric.
Hard negatives mined from BM25 top results train the transformer to correct BM25-specific errors, making the transformer-vs-BM25 comparison unfair and inflating the transformer's apparent advantage.
BM25 is denied access to features such as MeSH terms and recency that the transformer can exploit, so the comparison does not isolate semantic matching capability.
Session-level splitting still allows the same clinician to appear in both train and test, and the lab context feature can encode clinician-specific query patterns, creating user-level leakage.
The explicit annotation pool is drawn from clicked or surfaced documents only, so documents never surfaced by the current system are missing from labels and recall/nDCG may be optimistic.

The split strategy does not sort sessions chronologically, so future interactions can leak into training and evaluation, violating a prospective deployment simulation and enabling artificial gains from future-derived recency or lab-trend features.
Using MRR@10 as the primary metric discards lower-ranked relevant documents and does not reflect graded clinical relevance or top-10 coverage well.
Using claim-line count as the label targets utilization volume rather than the intended high-cost outcome, so the experiment tests a different construct than the hypothesis.
The transformer-vs-GBDT comparison is confounded because the transformer uses raw sequences while the GBM uses engineered aggregates; a like-for-like bag-of-codes baseline is missing.
The randomized-order ablation does not isolate temporal-order information from order-independent co-occurrence effects, so its interpretation is ambiguous.
Precision at 10% recall as a selection metric may favor threshold/calibration behavior and can overfit to the validation split.
The transformer may be disadvantaged by code vocabulary drift and rare-code handling across temporal splits, especially for low-frequency or emergent codes.
The baseline model is missing clinically standard features used in production risk models, making it an incomplete proxy for current practice.
Re-training the transformer on combined train and validation data changes the code vocabulary/effective model compared with the architecture chosen during validation.
The label is a within-period rank/top-decile target, so it may drift with cohort composition across temporal splits and depends on an unvalidated billing proxy.
The transformer hypothesis is under-supported because there is no logistic regression baseline and rare-code UNK behavior could hurt subpopulations.
The GBM baseline omits HCC scores and validated chronic condition flags.
A trivial prior-utilization rule baseline is absent.
Using claim line count as the target label measures billing volume/fragmentation rather than true high-cost utilization or clinical intensity.
The GBDT baseline is disadvantaged because it uses a much narrower engineered feature set than the transformer, confounding architecture with feature richness.
The randomized-order ablation does not isolate temporal modeling because the shuffled transformer can still exploit code co-occurrence / bag-of-codes information.
Hyperparameters selected on a validation window and then retraining on the same train+validation span may overfit that validation distribution or use an undefined early-stopping/selection procedure.
The sequence-length stratified analysis does not cleanly support claims about temporal reasoning because longer sequences are confounded with clinical complexity and recency effects.
Re-training the transformer on combined train+validation data gives it a larger-data advantage relative to the tuned GBM baseline.
The 20/80 validation split used for early stopping is ambiguous and could introduce mild leakage or unspecified model-selection bias.
The model’s advantages may not generalize if the cohort lacks sufficiently long, code-diverse sequences, so aggregate metrics may not test the intended hypothesis.
Excluding member IDs across splits may still allow population-level overlap because the same members recur across months, inflating prospective estimates.
Precision at exactly 10% recall is an unstable threshold-based criterion, especially when estimated on only a slice of the validation set.
Retraining the transformer on the combined train+validation set is not well validated because transformer training dynamics depend on dataset size and the early stopping criterion is undefined.
Using claim line count as the target measures utilization volume/billing frequency rather than high allowed-amount cost, so the task may not test the stated high-cost objective.
Excluding fixed-cost diagnoses is underdefined and may leave obvious high-utilization cases in the cohort, making the model appear better by recovering already-certain cases rather than latent risk.
The shuffled-order ablation may not isolate attention/sequence modeling because it should be compared against a non-attention pooled embedding baseline or set encoder baseline.
Hyperparameter tuning on the validation set across many configurations can upward-bias reported validation performance.
The primary metric precision at 10% recall is a single operating point that may not align with the deployment rule and may be unstable at low recall.
The randomized-order ablation may be confounded if day-offset information remains, allowing the model to infer sequence order.
The transformer may be evaluated in a short-sequence regime where it has little expected advantage, making a null result hard to interpret.
The temporal split does not separate chronic persistence from acute onset prediction, so a stratified first-time-positive analysis may be needed.
Calendar-period overlap between training labels and validation lookback features could cause population-level leakage via provider or geography features.
Right-censoring near the test-window boundary may bias labels downward for members with incomplete follow-up if not explicitly handled.
The randomized-order ablation cannot distinguish temporal sequence modeling from bag-of-codes co-occurrence modeling; a set-encoder ablation is needed.
The GBM baseline is disadvantaged because it uses only top-50 ICD-10 codes while the transformer uses the full learned vocabulary, so the comparison is confounded by feature budget.
Random stratified split lets future fraud patterns and fraud-ring/account identities leak into training, so evaluation is not a valid test of future-month fraud detection.
The GBT baseline is not given graph-derived/network features, so the comparison may be conflating graph-signal access with architectural superiority.
The 90-day temporal graph window is too short to capture meaningful fraud patterns.
The graph-based approach may not help on sparse/first-time-recipient fraud cases, so gains may come mainly from easier examples shared with the GBT.
ROC-AUC is an inappropriate primary metric under severe class imbalance because it can improve without meaningful gains at operational precision/recall points.
The ≥5 percentage-point AUC success threshold is not justified by a power analysis and may not correspond to meaningful operational improvement.
Device-sharing edges may be noisy, and the limited manual audit does not measure edge false-positive rate at scale or sensitivity to edge noise.
Device-sharing graph structure may encode fraud-ring relationships that help in-sample but may not persist when rings dissolve or migrate.
Stratified random split lets future fraud patterns and scam rings leak into training instead of using a strict chronological evaluation.
Using ROC-AUC as the main metric under extreme class imbalance does not capture operational performance at fixed precision/recall requirements.
Stratified random sampling within monthly buckets allows test accounts and edges to appear in the GNN training graph, causing structural leakage and inflated GNN performance.
The GNN operating mode is unspecified; if node ID embeddings are used, it may memorize node identities rather than learn transferable structure.
The 5-point AUC success threshold may be below detectable statistical power without a correlated-AUC significance test such as DeLong's.
The GNN gets baseline features plus graph embeddings while the GBT gets only baseline features, confounding architecture effects with feature-set advantages.
The split creates graph topology asymmetry because training graphs are sparser than test graphs when held-out edges are absent during training but present at evaluation.
The same accounts and device nodes appear in both train and test, allowing neighborhood aggregation to propagate fraud labels across the split and inflate GNN performance.
The experiment does not verify that fraudulent accounts cluster in the transaction/device graph, which is a prerequisite for the GNN to derive useful graph signal.
Global graph construction may expose future edges to test-time inference depending on implementation.
The 90-day label-completeness exclusion window causes recency bias and train/deploy mismatch that may affect the GNN more than the GBT.

Transaction-level random splitting lets the same accounts appear in both train and test, creating transductive leakage / account-memorization advantages for the GNN and invalidating future-month generalization claims.
The GBT baseline is not graph-informed, so the comparison may conflate the benefit of graph features with the benefit of learned message passing versus hand-engineered graph statistics.
Device-sharing edges may act as demographic proxy features and could induce disparate false-positive rates that a small manual audit would miss.
ROC-AUC is the wrong primary metric for highly imbalanced fraud detection because it can improve outside the operational high-precision/high-recall region without yielding practical benefit.
The 90-day rolling graph window makes early-period graphs systematically sparser, so apparent GNN gains may reflect graph-density differences rather than structural signal.
Splitting coordinated fraud clusters across transaction-level partitions can inflate precision-at-recall style metrics and hide the difficulty of the true operational case mix.
The hypothesis that fraud-positive nodes have enough neighborhood density for message passing to help may fail for sparse new-account APP fraud cases.
Device-sharing edges may encode investigator case-clustering or ring labels rather than independent fraud signals, making the audit insufficient to rule out inflated GNN performance on novel rings.
Using ECE after Platt scaling as the primary metric misaligns with the hypothesis about discrimination/ranking performance, and calibration-focused evaluation may obscure the intended effect.

Comparing a transformer on raw sequences against XGBoost on aggregated rolling-window features does not isolate architectural effects because the feature representation differs, so the performance gap is confounded.
The hybrid proxy label uses antibiotic orders and culture-related events that are also included as inputs, creating label-feature circularity or leakage that could advantage the sequence model.
A stratified random split across the full 36-month period can introduce temporal leakage because it does not enforce chronological separation between training and test data.
Sequence truncation to 512 events may disproportionately disadvantage high-acuity patients with denser event streams, biasing transformer evaluation in a clinically important subgroup.
Single-site validation limits mechanistic interpretation because any observed gain could reflect local documentation or workflow artifacts rather than true physiological signal.
XGBoost's multi-scale rolling-window features may approximate sequence-level signal, shrinking the intended architectural gap between models.
The transformer and XGBoost differ on architecture, representation, and inductive bias simultaneously, so the experiment cannot test the isolation hypothesis as designed.
Platt scaling may do more corrective work on transformer outputs than on XGBoost outputs, so calibration after scaling is not a neutral comparison of model quality.

ECE as the main metric is sensitive to binning, Platt scaling, and score distribution shape, making small differences hard to interpret and potentially masking the hypothesis of better sepsis prediction.
Using a stratified random split over the full time range rather than a chronological split can leak temporal information and inflate performance.

The transformer may have an unfair advantage because it ingests raw event sequences while XGBoost uses pre-aggregated rolling-window features, so the comparison mixes architecture with feature representation.

The sepsis label uses components such as qSOFA, lactate, organ dysfunction markers, antibiotics, and blood culture orders that are also model inputs, creating a circular label-definition problem and possible structural leakage.

The LSTM control is underspecified and may not receive the same event-tuple inputs as the transformer, making it hard to isolate whether any gain comes from attention over irregular sequences rather than representation or capacity.
Single-split bootstrap confidence intervals do not capture split-induced variability, so subgroup findings may not be robust.
Sequence truncation to 512 events may disproportionately affect dense-monitoring high-acuity patients, and the truncation policy is unspecified.
A global Platt scaling fit applied to both models may not correct subgroup- or site-specific miscalibration, so ECE differences could reflect calibration artifacts rather than architecture.
ECE as the main metric evaluates calibration rather than discrimination, so it may not test the claimed improvement in early sepsis prediction and could hide ranking errors.
Using a stratified split over the full study period can leak temporal information and should instead be a chronological split for realistic prospective evaluation.
The transformer's fixed-length truncated event sequences and discretized time-gap embeddings make it less distinct from XGBoost's windowed/tabular feature engineering than claimed.
The dual sepsis label definition may conflate treatment behavior or clinician suspicion with physiological state, creating label leakage/confounding through antibiotic-related features.
Truncating sequences to 512 events may unfairly disadvantage high-frequency or more severe patients by discarding earlier history in a non-random way.
The hyperparameter tuning budget may not be comparable across models, giving XGBoost a search-space or optimization advantage over the transformer.

XGBoost's feature set already encodes irregular sampling via counts, missingness, and multi-window statistics, reducing the experiment's ability to isolate an architectural advantage for the transformer.
The sepsis label's ±4 hour suspicion window may allow post-anchor clinical actions to influence label assignment, introducing look-ahead bias.
Splitting data across time can mask secular trends and concept drift that would matter in prospective deployment.
Temporal leakage from non-time-respecting train/test splitting across the full log, mixing future interaction patterns into training and inflating offline results.
Per-impression AUROC is misaligned with the goal of ranking the right items near the top within each session, and may reward global popularity discrimination instead of session-specific ordering.
Asymmetric positive and negative labeling windows can create boundary artifacts and inconsistent labels near the cutoff times.
The reported metric does not isolate whether any TGNN gain comes specifically from within-session temporal dynamics or session-depth effects.
The temporal split weakens the intended comparison because it does not really test the baseline's inability to adapt to time-varying distribution shift versus the TGNN's temporal advantage.
Item popularity or impression-count features may leak future information if computed in batch from the training fold rather than point-in-time from the event log.
Global graph propagation could aggregate edges from future periods into early test sessions, leaking future structure into evaluation.
Session-level random stratified sampling across the full 12-month log leaks future interaction patterns into training, especially for time-sensitive features like popularity and recency.
A 30-minute positive window with a 24-hour negative exclusion zone removes delayed-engagement users and may bias the evaluation toward immediate within-session signals.
Future sessions appearing in training via shuffle split leak future behavioral signal into model parameters, asymmetrically affecting the TGNN.
Time-windowed popularity features computed relative to impression timestamps may be distorted when sessions are shuffled across time.
The TGNN's temporal message-passing architecture may be under-validated if most sessions contain only 1–2 impressions, so gains may come from other factors.
Position bias correction is structurally weaker for SVD because positional confounding is embedded into latent factors before correction is applied.
Session-level random stratification plus time-windowed feature computation can cause lookahead leakage if later interactions contaminate training-set features for earlier sessions.
Per-impression AUROC is misaligned with the goal of ranking relevant items in top session-specific positions and may favor globally popular items over session-specific ordering.
Session-level stratified split across the full 12 months leaks future interaction patterns into training, contaminating temporal features and inflating offline performance.
The 24-hour negative-label window induces session-structure-correlated label noise that may let the TGNN exploit an artifact rather than true recommendation signal.
Including impression rank as a feature does not necessarily debias position effects and could let the model exploit position as a signal.
The SVD baseline with content-feature OOV initialization is a hybrid model rather than pure static matrix factorization, making the comparison confounded.
TGNN may be evaluated with future-dated edges under the stratified split, creating an inference-time temporal leakage/misconfiguration that would not be reproducible in production.
The impression pool is shaped by the production ranker's policy, introducing selection bias and potentially causing the model to imitate incumbent ranking behavior rather than user preferences.
AUROC is misaligned with top-of-list ranking quality; improvements in AUROC alone do not demonstrate better session-specific recommendation ranking.
Temporal leakage from session-stratified sampling asymmetrically benefits the TGNN because it can exploit time-window features while the baseline cannot.
