The reported TGNN gain may be driven only by dense-user/item segments; the experiment does not verify performance across sparse versus dense regimes.
Using click labels produced by the BM25 production ranker confounds evaluation because the learned ranker may reproduce BM25 behavior rather than learn independent relevance.
The stratified label budget is too small within query type × document age × month cells to support reliable claims about where gains are concentrated.
Resolving high-disagreement annotation pairs by majority vote but treating them with equal weight lets annotation noise influence outcomes.
Frozen clinical BERT embeddings may not transfer well to portal document vocabulary and style, making the semantic similarity feature uninformative for some documents.
Including impression rank as a feature encodes position bias rather than debiasing it.
Historical CTR features may leak relevance information across train/test if the query split is not exact enough.
Repeated hyperparameter tuning on a small validation set can overfit configuration choice to label noise.
Bootstrap confidence intervals computed at the query-document pair level underestimate variance because pairs within a query are correlated.
Historical CTR plus impression rank still leaves residual position bias, so popularity effects may be mistaken for relevance gains.
The ranker has a larger hyperparameter search space than BM25 relative to the validation budget, increasing overfitting risk.
The evaluation uses a query-level split that mixes observations from all calendar periods across train and test, allowing future document states and popularity patterns to leak into test evaluation.
Precision@10 uses binary judgments and discards graded relevance, so NDCG@10 would be a better fit for the stated objective.
Historical CTR as a training feature can create popularity/position-bias feedback loops and may cause the model to learn from click exposure rather than true clinical relevance.
The small manually labeled validation set for tuning and early stopping is too small for reliable Precision@10 estimates and may lead to noisy model selection.
Exact query-string splits may still allow semantically near-duplicate queries to cross train/test, inflating apparent generalization through shared CTR or interaction statistics.
Precision@10 treats graded relevance levels as binary and may miss gains in highly relevant documents; NDCG@10 would better reflect rank-sensitive graded relevance.
Impression rank used as a feature may be unusable at inference without a cascade architecture and can preserve BM25 ordering instead of correcting position bias.
The label budget may be insufficient for the planned stratified subgroup analyses, making subgroup confidence intervals too wide to support meaningful claims.
Precision@10 may be misaligned with physician lookup behavior, where shallow-rank metrics like MRR could be more appropriate.
Comparing the proposed system only against BM25 without intermediate baselines does not isolate the effect of click-data learning from simpler CTR-boosted or semantic reranking components.
Using historical CTR can leak document popularity across semantically similar queries that span train/test, making it hard to distinguish relevance learning from popularity exploitation.

Ranking StyleSpace channels by classifier sensitivity does not guarantee the resulting attributes are semantically interpretable rather than subtle, non-conceptual correlates.

User studies of perceptual plausibility or intuitive visual appeal do not establish faithfulness of the explanation to the classifier's decision mechanism.

StylEx explains the classifier only on the GAN-generated manifold, so attributes may not reflect behavior on natural images.
AttFind's greedy marginal channel search assumes attribute independence and can miss conjunctive or correlated features.
Because attribute discovery depends on the latent sample distribution used during search, results may be unrepresentative for underrepresented subgroups.
User studies may use curated counterfactual examples rather than random samples, inflating interpretability scores.
The method requires domain-specific generator training per task, undermining claims of broad practical applicability.
The explainer being trained jointly with the classifier makes it unclear whether discovered attributes reflect classifier logic or generator traversal directions.
GAN inversion fidelity on real images is not validated with a zero-edit baseline, so observed output changes may partly come from inversion distortion.
User interpretability studies mainly measure the visual plausibility of StyleGAN2 edits, which may be explained by pre-existing disentanglement rather than classifier integration.
On perceived-age classifiers, the paper conflates detecting a spurious feature with producing a valid semantic explanation of that classifier.

The method's generalization claims depend on the classifier behaving consistently on StyleGAN2's manifold, which may break under domain shift and beyond the face tasks evaluated.
StylEx lacks sufficient methodological detail to reproduce the architecture and training setup from the paper alone.
The paper's broad applicability claims are not well supported because verification was limited and only the perceived-age case was reproduced with available artifacts.
Joint training means the explainer reflects the classifier's behavior on the generator manifold rather than the original natural-image classifier behavior.
Lossy inversion into StyleGAN2/StyleSpace can omit classifier-relevant information, making explanations incomplete.

Poisoning rate was not swept across a realistic range to establish practical viability on small datasets.

Multiple comparisons across datasets, metrics, and attack variants were not corrected for.

The anchoring attack may not specifically target fairness because poisoned points may not occupy demographically asymmetric or boundary-relevant regions; observed fairness damage could just come from general accuracy degradation.
The influence-function-based attack may rely on an approximation/Hessian condition that was not validated on these small datasets and classifiers, so the optimization target may be miscalibrated.

COMPAS may be an unusually easy or non-representative target because of its structural properties, limiting generality.

Reported fairness metrics are point estimates without confidence intervals or multi-seed averaging, making it unclear whether effects exceed random variation.
There is no mechanism guaranteeing simultaneous degradation of both SPD and EOD, so reporting both may enable metric-selective presentation.
The datasets are small tabular benchmarks with label quality issues, so the poisoning rates may be unrealistically high to produce measurable fairness degradation.
The paper did not provide enough methodological detail to reproduce the experimental procedure without the released code.
Dataset reconstruction required extra assumptions and altered dataset versions because the written paper did not fully specify dataset preparation and preprocessing.

The evaluation is too weak because it lacks robustness baselines or defenses such as fairness-constrained retraining or sanitization, so attack non-triviality is unclear.
SPD/EOD changes may be confounded by accuracy degradation, so the paper does not clearly separate discriminatory behavior from uniformly worse performance.

The attacks may exploit dataset-specific sensitive-attribute correlations rather than demonstrating a general fairness poisoning mechanism.
Small subgroup test sets make SPD/EOD estimates high-variance, so confidence intervals or repeated-split statistics are needed to determine whether differences are real.

Baselines may not have been tuned or evaluated under the same fairness-degradation objective and constraints, making comparisons potentially unfair.
